whyWrongByChoice.A: Supine position minimizes the hydrostatic gradient between the head and heart, reducing air embolism risk compared to sitting.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Prone position does not create the same degree of venous subatmospheric pressure as the sitting position.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Left lateral decubitus does not create a significant above-heart hydrostatic gradient.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Trendelenburg (head-down) would increase venous pressure at the head, actually reducing air entry risk.

questions.stem: For a patient with amniotic fluid embolism who is hemodynamically unstable and has DIC, which of the following is the most appropriate initial management?

choices.label: B
choices.text: CPR, IV fluids, vasopressors, and blood product transfusion

explanation.summary: Unstable amniotic fluid embolism requires CPR, IV fluids, vasopressors (if hypotension persists), and blood product transfusions (platelets, FFP, cryoprecipitate) for DIC. Emergent cesarean may be needed if the fetus is undelivered.
explanation.whyCorrect: For unstable patients with amniotic fluid embolism, management includes: CPR if in cardiac arrest, IV fluids for volume resuscitation, vasopressors if hypotension persists despite fluids, and blood product transfusions (platelets, FFP, cryoprecipitate) for DIC. If the fetus has not been delivered, emergent cesarean delivery may be pursued.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Discharge is absolutely inappropriate for this life-threatening emergency.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Anticoagulation with heparin would worsen bleeding in a patient with DIC and consumption coagulopathy.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hyperbaric oxygen is used for air embolism, not amniotic fluid embolism.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Corticosteroids are not the primary treatment for amniotic fluid embolism.

questions.stem: Mitral valve endocarditis is the cardiac valve most likely to cause which of the following embolic complications?

explanation.summary: Mitral valve endocarditis (left-sided) is most likely to cause systemic (arterial) emboli because vegetations on the mitral valve fragment and enter the systemic arterial circulation, causing stroke, splenic/renal infarcts, and peripheral ischemia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Mitral valve endocarditis is most likely to cause systemic emboli because the mitral valve is on the left side of the heart. Vegetations that fragment from the mitral valve enter the systemic arterial circulation, potentially causing stroke, splenic infarcts, renal infarcts, and peripheral arterial ischemia. In contrast, right-sided endocarditis (tricuspid) causes pulmonary emboli.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Pulmonary emboli from endocarditis occur with right-sided (tricuspid) valve involvement, not mitral. Mitral valve fragments enter the systemic arterial circulation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Fat emboli originate from bone marrow after fractures, not from endocarditis vegetations.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Air emboli originate from medical procedures or diving, not endocarditis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Amniotic fluid emboli occur during labor and delivery, not from endocarditis.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with a history of anterior myocardial infarction two years ago presents with progressive dyspnea, lower-extremity edema, and weight gain. BP is 124/70 mm Hg, lungs are clear, and he has 1+ peripheral edema. You note a loud P2 but no S3. Echocardiography shows LVEF 30% and pulmonary artery pressure of 42 mm Hg. Which WHO group best classifies his pulmonary hypertension?

explanation.summary: Post-MI systolic heart failure causes Group 2 (post-capillary) PH.
explanation.whyCorrect: Left heart failure raises left atrial pressure, transmitted backward to pulmonary veins and capillaries, causing Group 2 PH.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Group 1 (PAH) involves intrinsic arterial disease, not LV dysfunction.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Group 3 is from chronic lung disease with hypoxemic vasoconstriction.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Group 4 is from chronic thromboembolism.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Group 5 is multifactorial/unclear causes like sickle cell or sarcoidosis.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman from rural Egypt presents with progressive dyspnea and fatigue. She recalls swimming in freshwater canals as a child. Echocardiogram suggests elevated pulmonary artery pressures. Stool examination reveals parasitic eggs. Which mechanism most directly explains her pulmonary hypertension?

explanation.summary: Schistosomiasis is the leading worldwide cause of PH; eggs embolize to pulmonary arterioles.
explanation.whyCorrect: Schistosomal eggs travel as emboli into the pulmonary circulation and lodge in arterioles, increasing pulmonary vascular resistance.

whyWrongByChoice.A: BMPR2 mutations cause heritable PAH, not parasitic PH.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Hypoxemic vasoconstriction describes Group 3 lung disease.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Post-capillary transmission describes Group 2 left heart disease.
whyWrongByChoice.E: CTEPH (Group 4) involves organized fibrotic thrombi, not parasite eggs.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old woman with idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension is started on bosentan. Which adverse effect most requires scheduled laboratory monitoring while on this drug?

explanation.summary: Bosentan causes dose-dependent hepatotoxicity requiring LFT monitoring.
explanation.whyCorrect: Bosentan is associated with hepatotoxicity; baseline and periodic liver function monitoring is required per labeling.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Thyroid dysfunction is not a bosentan-associated toxicity.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Nephrotoxicity is not the main labeled concern with bosentan.
whyWrongByChoice.D: QT prolongation is not a hallmark bosentan effect.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Pulmonary fibrosis is not a bosentan-associated toxicity.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with severe PAH on continuous IV epoprostenol via central catheter is admitted for line infection. The infusion line becomes dislodged and therapy is abruptly interrupted. Which complication is most likely?

explanation.summary: Abrupt cessation of IV prostacyclin causes rebound PH and decompensation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Abrupt interruption of continuous prostacyclin therapy, particularly IV epoprostenol, can precipitate rebound pulmonary hypertension and rapid clinical decompensation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Not associated with prostacyclin withdrawal.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Priapism is linked to PDE5 inhibitors, not prostacyclins.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Not related to prostacyclin therapy.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Not related to prostacyclin therapy.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old man with PAH is managed with sildenafil. He is started on riociguat by a new provider. Which adverse effect is he at greatest risk of?

explanation.summary: Combining riociguat with PDE5 inhibitors is contraindicated due to severe hypotension.
explanation.whyCorrect: Riociguat is contraindicated with PDE-5 inhibitors due to additive enhancement of the NO-cGMP pathway causing severe hypotension.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Hepatic failure is not the primary concern of this combination.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Both agents lower BP, so hypertensive emergency is opposite of what occurs.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Pulmonary edema is not the characteristic effect.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Thrombocytosis is not associated.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with severe COPD (FEV1 28% predicted) has chronic hypoxemia and progressive dyspnea. Echo reveals RV hypertrophy and estimated PAP of 48 mm Hg. Which mechanism most directly accounts for his pulmonary hypertension?

explanation.summary: COPD-related hypoxemia causes reflex pulmonary arteriolar vasoconstriction (Group 3).
explanation.whyCorrect: Hypoxemia in COPD causes reflex pulmonary arteriole vasoconstriction, increasing vascular resistance and PAP.

whyWrongByChoice.A: BMPR2 mutation causes heritable Group 1 PAH.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Endothelin excess characterizes Group 1 PAH, not Group 3.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Organized thrombi define Group 4 CTEPH.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Elevated PCWP defines Group 2 left heart disease.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman has a family history of early pulmonary hypertension. Genetic testing reveals a mutation in the BMPR2 gene. Which vascular change is most directly driven by this mutation?

explanation.summary: BMPR2 mutations drive pulmonary arterial endothelial cell proliferation and fibrosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Heritable PAH from BMPR2/ALK1 mutations causes pulmonary arterial endothelial cell proliferation and arterial wall fibrosis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Hypoxic vasoconstriction is Group 3 mechanism.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Alveolar hypoventilation is not the BMPR2 mechanism.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Venous congestion is Group 2 mechanism.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Organized thrombi characterize Group 4.

questions.stem: A patient with idiopathic PAH undergoes lung biopsy. Which histologic finding is most characteristic of advanced PAH?

explanation.summary: Plexiform lesions are hallmark of advanced PAH pathology.
explanation.whyCorrect: In PAH, small blood vessels proliferate within the media compressing the original lumen, forming plexiform lesions.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Caseating granulomas suggest TB.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Hyaline membranes are seen in ARDS.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Honeycombing is seen in pulmonary fibrosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Noncaseating granulomas are seen in sarcoidosis.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman has survived two pulmonary emboli in the past 3 years. She now presents with progressive exertional dyspnea. Echocardiogram suggests PH. CT pulmonary angiography shows organized fibrotic material in the pulmonary arteries. Which WHO group is this?

explanation.summary: CTEPH (Group 4) results from unresolved, organized pulmonary emboli.
explanation.whyCorrect: Chronic thromboembolism develops when acute PEs fail to resolve and instead organize into fibrotic obstructions, defining Group 4 PH.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Group 1 is intrinsic pulmonary arterial disease.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Group 2 is left heart disease.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Group 3 is parenchymal lung disease/hypoxia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Group 5 is multifactorial unclear causes.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old HIV-positive man with good viral control (undetectable viral load) develops progressive exertional dyspnea. Echocardiogram suggests PAH. What mechanism most likely underlies his pulmonary hypertension?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Direct viral replication in pulmonary arteries

explanation.summary: HIV-PAH is driven by viral protein-mediated endothelial injury, independent of viral load.
explanation.whyCorrect: HIV-associated PAH occurs via viral protein-mediated endothelial injury and chronic inflammatory mediators that drive pulmonary arterial remodeling; it is unrelated to viral load.

whyWrongByChoice.B: HIV-PAH is not due to direct viral replication in arterial wall.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Opportunistic infection is not the mechanism.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Antiretrovirals are not the cause.
whyWrongByChoice.E: CD4 count does not drive it.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman with idiopathic PAH has symptoms despite dual therapy. Echocardiogram is inconclusive. What is the gold-standard test to confirm PH and precisely measure hemodynamics?

explanation.summary: Right heart catheterization is gold standard for diagnosing PH.
explanation.whyCorrect: Right heart catheterization is the gold standard, measuring resting mean PAP (≥20 mm Hg = PH) and other hemodynamics.

whyWrongByChoice.A: HRCT evaluates parenchyma, not hemodynamics.
whyWrongByChoice.B: V/Q scans are used for CTEPH screening, not gold standard diagnosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cardiac MRI is adjunctive, not gold standard.
whyWrongByChoice.E: TEE images the heart but does not directly measure PA pressure invasively.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with PH has an ECG showing tall R waves in leads II, III, and aVF with an S wave in lead I, along with T-wave inversions in the inferior leads. What do these findings most likely represent?

explanation.summary: Right axis deviation with inferior strain reflects RV hypertrophy from PH.
explanation.whyCorrect: ECGs in PH may show RV hypertrophy with right axis deviation (positive QRS in II, III, aVF; negative in I) and strain pattern (T inversions/ST depressions inferiorly).

whyWrongByChoice.A: LBBB produces wide QRS with different morphology.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Anterior MI localizes to V1-V4.
whyWrongByChoice.D: AF shows irregularly irregular rhythm without P waves.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Posterior MI shows tall R in V1-V2.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman with PAH is on sildenafil. She takes nitroglycerin for presumed anginal chest pain and develops profound hypotension and syncope. What mechanism caused this reaction?

explanation.summary: Nitrates plus PDE5 inhibitors cause additive cGMP-mediated vasodilation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Both nitrates (NO donors) and PDE5 inhibitors increase cGMP in vascular smooth muscle; combined use causes profound hypotension/syncope.

whyWrongByChoice.A: CYP3A4 inhibition is not the mechanism of the hypotensive crisis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Beta blockade is not involved.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Alpha stimulation would raise BP.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Endothelin antagonism is the ERA mechanism, not PDE5.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman is being started on ambrisentan for PAH. Which fetal risk requires contraception counseling and routine pregnancy testing in this patient?

explanation.summary: ERAs are teratogenic, requiring strict contraception and pregnancy surveillance.
explanation.whyCorrect: ERAs are teratogenic, requiring reliable contraception and routine pregnancy testing when applicable.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Macrosomia is not an ERA effect.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Preterm labor is not a known ERA effect.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Fetal hypoglycemia is not an ERA effect.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Neonatal sepsis is not a known ERA effect.

questions.stem: A patient with idiopathic PAH is started on iloprost. What is the primary molecular signaling pathway activated by this agent?

explanation.summary: Prostacyclin analogs activate IP receptor, raising cAMP for vasodilation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Prostacyclin analogs bind the IP receptor on pulmonary vascular smooth muscle, activating adenylate cyclase to increase cAMP, lower calcium, and cause pulmonary vasodilation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Activating ETA would worsen PAH (this pathway is blocked by ERAs).
whyWrongByChoice.C: Riociguat works via sGC stimulation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: PDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil) raise cGMP.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Alpha-1 stimulation causes vasoconstriction.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman with idiopathic PAH for two months presents with worsening dyspnea, clear lungs, and a right-ventricular S3. Labs show elevated liver enzymes (not drug-induced). Which additional finding is most likely present?

explanation.summary: Right heart failure from PAH causes venous congestion producing hepatic congestion and ascites.
explanation.whyCorrect: Right heart failure causes elevated venous pressure leading to hepatic congestion (elevated LFTs) and ascites.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Orthopnea is typical of left-sided failure.
whyWrongByChoice.C: PND is a left-sided failure feature.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Pulmonary edema is left-sided failure; her lungs are clear.
whyWrongByChoice.E: An aortic ejection murmur is not expected in PAH right heart failure.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old woman with systemic sclerosis develops exertional dyspnea. Echocardiogram suggests elevated PAP. Which WHO group of PH does she most likely have?

explanation.summary: Connective tissue disease-associated PAH is Group 1 (secondary PAH).
explanation.whyCorrect: Secondary PAH due to inflammatory disorders (systemic sclerosis, lupus, dermatomyositis) is classified as Group 1.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Group 2 is left heart disease.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Group 3 is chronic lung disease/hypoxia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Group 4 is CTEPH.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Group 5 is miscellaneous/unclear causes.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man has methamphetamine-associated PAH. Molecular analysis of his pulmonary vasculature shows excess endothelin-1 signaling. Which effect of ET-1 at the ETA receptor most directly contributes to his disease?

explanation.summary: ET-1 at ETA receptors raises intracellular calcium, causing vasoconstriction and remodeling.
explanation.whyCorrect: ET-1 acts mainly at ETA receptors on pulmonary vascular smooth muscle to increase intracellular calcium, causing vasoconstriction and stimulating proliferation/fibrosis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: cAMP rise is the IP (prostacyclin) pathway.
whyWrongByChoice.C: cGMP rise is the NO/PDE5 pathway.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Platelet inhibition is a prostacyclin effect.
whyWrongByChoice.E: ET-1 drives fibroblast proliferation, not apoptosis.

questions.stem: A patient with severe PAH develops side effects while on epoprostenol including headache, flushing, jaw pain, and diarrhea. Which additional risk is most likely with this agent, especially when combined with anticoagulants?

explanation.summary: Prostacyclins inhibit platelet aggregation, raising bleeding risk.
explanation.whyCorrect: Prostacyclin agents inhibit platelet aggregation; bleeding risk can be clinically significant, especially with anticoagulants or thrombocytopenia.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Prostacyclins cause hypotension, not hypertension.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Hepatotoxicity is a bosentan (ERA) issue.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Renal tubular necrosis is not a prostacyclin toxicity.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Hyperkalemia is not associated with prostacyclins.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with long-standing untreated pulmonary hypertension now has marked JVD, ascites, and a holosystolic murmur at the left lower sternal border that increases with inspiration. Which cardiac change underlies these findings?

explanation.summary: Chronic PH leads to RVH and failure with TR murmur (Carvallo sign) and congestion.
explanation.whyCorrect: Chronic PH increases RV afterload, producing RV hypertrophy and failure; inspiration-worsening murmur reflects tricuspid regurgitation, plus JVD and ascites from venous congestion.

whyWrongByChoice.A: LVH does not cause an inspiration-worsening TR murmur.
whyWrongByChoice.B: MVP causes a mid-systolic click, not an inspiration-augmented murmur.
whyWrongByChoice.C: HCM causes a murmur that worsens with Valsalva, not inspiration.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Aortic stenosis is a crescendo-decrescendo murmur at the right second interspace.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with PH and severe ongoing hypoxemia despite optimized medical therapy remains symptomatic. Which adjunctive intervention helps minimize hypoxemia-mediated pulmonary arteriolar vasoconstriction?

explanation.summary: Oxygen therapy treats hypoxemia and reduces reflex pulmonary vasoconstriction.
explanation.whyCorrect: Patients with persistent symptoms may benefit from oxygen therapy to treat hypoxemia and minimize hypoxemia-mediated vasoconstriction of pulmonary arterioles.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Loop diuretic escalation alone does not target hypoxic vasoconstriction.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Corticosteroids are not a PH-specific therapy.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Heparin is not a general PH adjunct for hypoxic vasoconstriction.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Bronchodilators do not primarily address hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction.

questions.stem: A patient with PAH is found to have plexiform lesions, neointimal fibrosis, and in situ thrombi on autopsy. Which sequence best represents the pathologic progression in PAH?

explanation.summary: PAH progresses: vasoconstriction, remodeling/inflammation, plexiform lesions, then thrombotic lesions.
explanation.whyCorrect: The described pathologic steps in PAH are: (1) vasoconstriction, (2) arterial remodeling and inflammation, (3) plexiform lesions, and (4) thrombotic lesions.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Thrombosis is a late, not initial, step.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Vasoconstriction precedes remodeling.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Plexiform lesions are not the initial change.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Remodeling precedes both plexiform and thrombotic lesions.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old woman develops acute dyspnea 4 days after left hip replacement. Pulse 120/min, BP 100/70 mm Hg, RR 30/min, SpO2 92%. Lungs are clear; her left calf is swollen and tender. Which combination best explains her hypoxemia?

explanation.summary: PE hypoxemia is caused by low V/Q mismatch, not dead space.
explanation.whyCorrect: Blood is diverted to healthy alveoli (increased Q) without proportional ventilation, causing venous admixture and hypoxemia.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Dead space has only a minor effect on PaO2.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Diffusion limitation is not the mechanism in acute PE.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Shunt physiology is not typical of PE.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Patient is hyperventilating, not hypoventilating.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman presents with pleuritic chest pain and mild dyspnea. She has no hemoptysis, no prior DVT, no recent surgery, no leg swelling, no estrogen use. HR 88, SpO2 98% on room air, age <50. Wells score is 0. What is the most appropriate next step?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Apply PERC rule to exclude PE

explanation.summary: In low-risk patients, PERC criteria can exclude PE without further testing.
explanation.whyCorrect: If all PERC criteria are met in a low-risk patient, PE probability is <2% and no further testing is needed.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Unnecessary if PERC is negative in a low-risk patient.
whyWrongByChoice.C: CTA is overuse in PERC-negative low-risk patients.
whyWrongByChoice.D: V/Q scan is not indicated here.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Anticoagulation is not given without diagnosis in a low-risk patient.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man with active colon cancer presents with dyspnea and tachycardia (HR 110). He has unilateral leg swelling. Wells score is 6. Which is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?

explanation.summary: High pretest probability (Wells >4) requires CT angiography.
explanation.whyCorrect: Patients with high PE suspicion skip D-dimer and proceed directly to CTA, the imaging test of choice.

whyWrongByChoice.A: D-dimer is not useful in high-probability patients due to low specificity.
whyWrongByChoice.B: CXR is never diagnostic for PE.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Echo detects RV strain but does not diagnose PE.
whyWrongByChoice.E: PERC applies only to low-risk patients.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old pregnant woman at 28 weeks gestation presents with pleuritic chest pain and mild dyspnea. HR 108, SpO2 96%. She has no leg swelling. D-dimer is elevated. Which imaging is most appropriate given her pregnancy?

explanation.summary: MRI avoids radiation and is increasingly used in pregnancy.
explanation.whyCorrect: Lung MRI avoids radiation exposure and its role is increasing in pregnant patients with suspected PE.

whyWrongByChoice.A: CTA involves radiation and iodinated contrast.
whyWrongByChoice.B: CXR is never diagnostic for PE.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Abdominal ultrasound does not evaluate for PE.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Invasive pulmonary arteriography is rarely used.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with a known iodinated contrast allergy and stable creatinine has intermediate suspicion for PE. CTA is contraindicated. Which alternative imaging best establishes the diagnosis?

explanation.summary: V/Q scan is an alternative when CTA cannot be performed.
explanation.whyCorrect: V/Q scan can diagnose PE when contrast is contraindicated; mismatch (normal ventilation, absent perfusion) suggests PE.

whyWrongByChoice.B: CXR is not diagnostic.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Echo detects RV strain, not the embolus directly.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Doppler finds DVT but does not rule in PE.
whyWrongByChoice.E: D-dimer alone cannot confirm PE.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with acute PE has BP 80/50 mm Hg despite IV fluids. Echo shows severe RV dilation. He has no contraindications to thrombolysis. Which is the most appropriate immediate intervention?

explanation.summary: Hemodynamic instability is the indication for systemic fibrinolysis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hemodynamically unstable PE without contraindications warrants systemic tPA to rapidly reduce clot burden.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Anticoagulation alone is insufficient in shock.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Warfarin has delayed onset and is not used alone initially.
whyWrongByChoice.D: IVC filter prevents new emboli but does not treat current shock.
whyWrongByChoice.E: DOACs do not dissolve existing clot rapidly.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman with a stable PE had a hemorrhagic stroke 2 weeks ago. She cannot be anticoagulated. Which intervention is most appropriate?

explanation.summary: IVC filters are used when anticoagulation is contraindicated.
explanation.whyCorrect: Patients with PE who cannot be anticoagulated due to high bleeding risk can have an IVC filter placed to prevent further embolization.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Any anticoagulant is contraindicated after recent hemorrhagic stroke.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Fibrinolysis dramatically worsens bleeding risk.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Aspirin is inadequate and still raises bleeding risk.
whyWrongByChoice.E: All anticoagulants are contraindicated.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman with acute PE is stable (HR 92, BP 128/78, SpO2 96% on RA). Troponin is normal; echo shows no RV strain. Renal function is normal. Which initial agent is most appropriate as monotherapy?

explanation.summary: DOACs like apixaban are appropriate initial monotherapy for stable PE.
explanation.whyCorrect: Apixaban is a factor Xa inhibitor and a first-line DOAC option for immediate anticoagulation in stable PE.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Warfarin cannot be used alone initially due to delayed onset.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Aspirin is not an anticoagulant.
whyWrongByChoice.D: tPA is reserved for unstable/massive PE.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Clopidogrel is an antiplatelet, not appropriate for PE.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with acute PE has BP 120/78, HR 96, SpO2 95%. Troponin is elevated and echo shows RV dilation without hypotension. What is the most appropriate management?

explanation.summary: Intermediate-risk PE: anticoagulate and monitor closely.
explanation.whyCorrect: Intermediate-risk PE (RV dysfunction without hypotension) should generally be treated like low-risk with close monitoring for deterioration and expert consultation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Routine thrombolysis is not recommended without hemodynamic instability.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Reserved for unstable patients who fail or cannot receive fibrinolytics.
whyWrongByChoice.D: IVC filter is not first-line when anticoagulation is possible.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Observation alone risks deterioration.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old woman on combined oral contraceptives has a suspected PE. Arterial blood gas during tachypnea is most likely to show which pattern?

explanation.summary: Hyperventilation in PE causes respiratory alkalosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hypoxemia drives increased minute ventilation, producing hypocarbia and respiratory alkalosis classically associated with PE.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Hypercarbia is very uncommon because ventilation remains effective.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Not the primary acid-base disturbance in acute PE.
whyWrongByChoice.D: PaCO2 typically falls, not rises.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Not the expected pattern in acute PE.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man presents with sudden dyspnea and pleuritic chest pain. CXR shows a peripheral wedge-shaped opacification. Which finding does this represent?

explanation.summary: Hampton's hump is a peripheral wedge-shaped opacity from lung infarct.
explanation.whyCorrect: A peripheral wedge-shaped opacification due to pulmonary infarct is called Hampton's hump, an uncommon but classic PE finding.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Westermark sign is regional decreased vascularity, not a wedge opacity.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Kerley B lines reflect interstitial edema.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Air bronchograms suggest consolidation/pneumonia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Ground-glass opacities are nonspecific.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with massive PE is hypotensive. Echo shows a dilated right ventricle bowing the intraventricular septum into the left ventricle. This finding most directly explains which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reduced LV preload and cardiac output

explanation.summary: RV dilation compresses the LV, reducing preload and output.
explanation.whyCorrect: RV dilation from obstruction impairs LV filling, reducing LV stroke volume and cardiac output (obstructive shock).

whyWrongByChoice.B: LV contractility is not primarily increased in PE.
whyWrongByChoice.C: RA pressure rises, not falls.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Coronary perfusion typically worsens with hypotension.
whyWrongByChoice.E: PA pressure rises because of the obstruction.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old bedridden woman with a hip fracture develops sudden dyspnea, HR 125, BP 90/60, SpO2 88%. Which is the most likely precipitating factor for her PE?

explanation.summary: Prolonged immobilization causes venous stasis and DVT/PE.
explanation.whyCorrect: Bedrest from hip fracture causes venous stasis (a Virchow's triad component) leading to DVT and PE.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Age raises risk but is not the proximate precipitant.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Not indicated in this scenario.
whyWrongByChoice.D: No travel history is given.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Not mentioned; age makes it unlikely.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man is admitted with confirmed PE. The attending wants to start anticoagulation before imaging due to high clinical suspicion. Which of the following best supports this practice?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Empirical anticoagulation is recommended when PE likelihood is high

choices.label: D
choices.text: D-dimer is falsely low in PE

choices.label: E
choices.text: Anticoagulation eliminates the need for imaging

explanation.summary: Start anticoagulation empirically when PE is highly likely.
explanation.whyCorrect: If the likelihood of PE is high after history/exam, anticoagulation should not be delayed awaiting imaging.

whyWrongByChoice.A: DOACs prevent new clot formation; natural fibrinolysis continues.
whyWrongByChoice.B: CTA has high sensitivity and specificity.
whyWrongByChoice.D: D-dimer is typically elevated in PE.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Imaging is still required to confirm the diagnosis.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with acute PE remains hypotensive despite fluids and has absolute contraindications to systemic thrombolytics (recent intracranial hemorrhage). Which is the next most appropriate intervention?

explanation.summary: Embolectomy is indicated when fibrinolytics are contraindicated.
explanation.whyCorrect: Catheter-based or surgical embolectomy is indicated in critically ill patients who cannot receive fibrinolytics.

whyWrongByChoice.A: DOACs do not lyse large existing thrombi.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Aspirin is inadequate in massive PE.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Warfarin has delayed onset.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Oxygen alone does not address clot burden.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman has Wells score 3. She has no hemoptysis or estrogen use but is HR 102. D-dimer is mildly elevated. What is the best next step?

explanation.summary: Low-to-moderate suspicion with elevated D-dimer warrants CTA.
explanation.whyCorrect: Chest CTA should be performed in patients with low-to-moderate suspicion and elevated D-dimer, as well as in high-suspicion patients.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Elevated D-dimer with PE symptoms cannot be dismissed.
whyWrongByChoice.C: tPA requires confirmed diagnosis and instability.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Repeating D-dimer does not change management.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Warfarin alone is never appropriate initial therapy.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man recovers from an acute PE after 3 months of anticoagulation. Which complication occurs in approximately 1% to 5% of PE survivors and is characterized by persistent clot burden?

explanation.summary: CTEPH develops in 1-5% of acute PE patients.
explanation.whyCorrect: Some patients (1-5%) develop persistent pulmonary thrombi causing chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension and right heart failure.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Not associated with PE.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Not a typical PE complication.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Unrelated to embolism.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Not caused by PE.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman with stable PE has severe chronic kidney disease (CKD) and iodinated contrast allergy. Which is the best diagnostic imaging?

explanation.summary: MRI avoids iodinated contrast and radiation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Lung MRI is an alternative for patients with iodine allergy or severe CKD who cannot receive CTA.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Contraindicated by allergy and renal dysfunction.
whyWrongByChoice.C: CXR is not diagnostic.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Invasive catheter angiography is rarely used.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Abdominal CT does not image the lungs adequately.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old trauma patient develops PE. The ICU team administers catheter-directed rather than systemic thrombolysis. Which is the primary rationale?

explanation.summary: Catheter-directed lysis minimizes systemic drug exposure and bleeding.
explanation.whyCorrect: Delivering fibrinolytics directly to the embolus reduces systemic exposure and bleeding risk compared with systemic therapy.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Clot lysis efficacy is not dramatically different.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Catheter procedures are not cheaper.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Anticoagulation is still given afterward.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Not the main reason for directed therapy.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old woman presents after a transatlantic flight. Which element of Virchow's triad is most directly implicated in her DVT risk?

explanation.summary: Prolonged travel causes venous stasis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Prolonged immobility during travel slows venous return, producing stasis, a component of Virchow's triad.

whyWrongByChoice.A: No direct venous trauma in travel alone.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hypercoagulability requires another inciting factor.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Not a primary mechanism in travel-related DVT.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Not the primary factor described.

questions.stem: A 33-year-old woman presents 2 days postpartum with sudden dyspnea, cyanosis, hypotension, and DIC. Which embolus type is most likely?

explanation.summary: Amniotic fluid embolus occurs during/after parturition.
explanation.whyCorrect: Amniotic fluid embolism arises from obstetric complications during parturition with sudden cardiovascular collapse and DIC.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Possible but less specific to the scenario.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Fat embolus follows long-bone fracture.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Air embolus follows rapid ascent or vascular procedures.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Tumor embolism is rare and not acute.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman with confirmed PE is started on enoxaparin. Her care team plans long-term therapy. What is the typical outpatient duration of anticoagulation after PE?

explanation.summary: Standard outpatient anticoagulation duration after PE is 3-12 months.
explanation.whyCorrect: Per guidelines, anticoagulation is continued for 3 to 12 months using DOACs, enoxaparin, dabigatran, fondaparinux, or warfarin.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Too short to prevent recurrence.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Less than the recommended 3-12 months.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Lifelong therapy is reserved for specific patients only.
whyWrongByChoice.E: D-dimer is not used to guide duration.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man underwent open reduction and internal fixation of a femur fracture 48 hours ago. He now has tachypnea, confusion, hypoxemia (SpO2 86%), and a red-brown petechial rash over his chest and neck. Chest CT angiography shows no filling defects. What is the most likely underlying mechanism?

explanation.summary: Fat embolism syndrome classic triad 24-72 hours post-long bone fracture.
explanation.whyCorrect: Femur marrow fat enters ruptured venules, lodges in lungs, and degrades into free fatty acids causing the classic triad of dyspnea, neurologic deficits, and petechial rash.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Thromboembolism would show CT filling defects and lacks petechial rash.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Air emboli require vascular communication and pressure gradient, not fracture.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Amniotic fluid embolism occurs peripartum, not post-trauma.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Septic emboli require endocarditis source with fever and vegetations.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old woman becomes acutely dyspneic, hypotensive, and develops uterine hemorrhage 10 minutes after vaginal delivery. Labs show platelets 40,000, prolonged INR, and low fibrinogen. She is afebrile. Which pathophysiologic mechanism is primarily responsible for her hemodynamic collapse?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Air lodging in right ventricular outflow

explanation.summary: Amniotic fluid embolism causes vasogenic pulmonary hypertension, not mechanical obstruction.
explanation.whyCorrect: Fetal antigenic debris triggers pulmonary vasoconstriction leading to acute right ventricular failure, cardiogenic shock, plus procoagulant activation causing DIC.

whyWrongByChoice.A: AFE consequences arise from antigenic properties, not mechanical blockage.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Free fatty acids characterize fat embolism, not AFE.
whyWrongByChoice.D: No procedural history of air entry; AFE explains DIC.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Patient is afebrile without endocarditis risk factors.

questions.stem: A neurosurgeon is performing a posterior fossa craniotomy on a patient in the sitting position. Suddenly, the patient's end-tidal CO2 drops and blood pressure falls. A central line is in place. What is the primary mechanism enabling this complication?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Paradoxical thrombus through patent foramen ovale

choices.label: B
choices.text: Marrow fat release from bony drilling

explanation.summary: Venous air embolism during sitting neurosurgery due to hydrostatic pressure gradient.
explanation.whyCorrect: When the surgical site is above the heart, head/neck venous pressure can drop below atmospheric pressure, allowing air entry through open veins.

whyWrongByChoice.A: PFO allows paradoxical air passage but is not the primary entry mechanism.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Cranial bone marrow is not the fat-rich source implicated in fat embolism.
whyWrongByChoice.D: No obstetric context present.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Catheter fragmentation is a rare foreign body cause, not the sitting-position mechanism.

questions.stem: A scuba diver rapidly ascends from 40 meters without exhaling after his regulator malfunctions. He surfaces with acute chest pain, hemiparesis, and altered mental status. Which mechanism best explains his stroke?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Nitrogen gas coming out of solution in veins

choices.label: B
choices.text: Alveolar rupture allowing arterial air entry

explanation.summary: Pulmonary barotrauma from breath-holding ascent causes arterial air embolism and stroke.
explanation.whyCorrect: Ascent without exhalation causes alveolar rupture; air enters pulmonary veins and then arterial circulation, causing stroke/MI due to large volumes entering arteries.

whyWrongByChoice.A: That mechanism (decompression sickness) typically affects joints/muscles/lungs with slower onset.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Thrombus formation is not characteristic of rapid ascent.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Fat emboli require long-bone fracture.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Focal neurologic deficits indicate embolic occlusion, not diffuse vasospasm.

questions.stem: A nurse inadvertently disconnects a central venous catheter while the patient sits upright. The patient immediately becomes dyspneic and hypotensive. In which position should the patient be placed?

explanation.summary: Venous air embolism management: left lateral decubitus traps air in right ventricle.
explanation.whyCorrect: Left lateral decubitus positioning traps air in the right ventricle apex, preventing migration into pulmonary arteries.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Head elevation worsens venous air entry pathophysiology.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Prone positioning is not indicated for venous air embolism.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Right lateral would push air toward the pulmonary outflow.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Reverse Trendelenburg increases risk of further air entry.

questions.stem: A diver presents with severe chest pain, hypotension, and confusion after rapid ascent. Supplemental oxygen is initiated. Which definitive therapy is most appropriate?

explanation.summary: Severe scuba-related air embolism requires hyperbaric oxygen for definitive treatment.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hyperbaric chambers (2-3x atmospheric pressure) compress air bubbles and increase tissue oxygen delivery to ischemic regions.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Anticoagulation does not dissolve air bubbles.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Thrombolytics target fibrin, not air.
whyWrongByChoice.D: This is used for venous, not scuba-related arterial air embolism (supine preferred).
whyWrongByChoice.E: iNO addresses pulmonary hypertension, not bubble dissolution.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man with a tibial and pelvic fracture develops dyspnea, confusion, and a petechial rash on his anterior chest 36 hours post-injury. Why are these particular fractures high-risk for this syndrome?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased platelet activation at fracture sites

explanation.summary: Long bone and pelvic marrow contains abundant fat and vascular supply.
explanation.whyCorrect: These bones have marrow particularly rich in fat and are highly vascularized, allowing fat to enter ruptured venules and reach the lungs.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Platelet activation causes thromboembolism, not fat embolism.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Bacterial colonization causes osteomyelitis/septic emboli, not fat embolism.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Tissue factor release alone does not explain fat globule embolization.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Cortical porosity is not the key factor; marrow composition is.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old IV drug user presents with fever, dyspnea, and bilateral lung abscesses on CT. Blood cultures grow Staphylococcus aureus. Echocardiography shows a vegetation. Which valve is most likely affected as the source of emboli in this presentation?

explanation.summary: IV drug use with septic pulmonary emboli indicates tricuspid valve endocarditis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Tricuspid valve endocarditis in IV drug users sends septic emboli to the lungs (venous circulation), causing multiple cavitary abscesses.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Aortic emboli go to systemic arterial circulation, not lungs.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Pulmonic endocarditis is rare and not the typical IV drug use source.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Mitral is most likely overall and for systemic emboli, but septic pulmonary emboli in IVDU are from tricuspid.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The eustachian valve is a vestigial structure not relevant here.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with known hepatocellular carcinoma develops progressive dyspnea, tachycardia, and signs of pulmonary hypertension over several weeks. Chest CT shows no large vessel filling defects but scattered small nodules. What is the most likely embolic mechanism?

explanation.summary: HCC characteristically invades blood vessels producing tumor emboli.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hepatocellular and renal cell carcinomas invade vessels; tumor fragments lodge in the lungs, cause thrombi/inflammation, and can lead to pulmonary hypertension.

whyWrongByChoice.A: No fracture described; fat embolism presents acutely.
whyWrongByChoice.C: No fever or abscesses described.
whyWrongByChoice.D: No procedural history.
whyWrongByChoice.E: CT would typically show large-vessel filling defects for large PE.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old IV drug user has chronic dyspnea. Lung biopsy shows granulomatous inflammation surrounding birefringent crystalline material. Which embolus type is most consistent?

explanation.summary: Talc contamination from injected drugs causes granulomatous foreign body emboli.
explanation.whyCorrect: Injected street drugs may contain talc/cotton/cellulose; these foreign materials lodge in the lungs and incite granulomatous (walling off) inflammation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Fat embolism does not produce birefringent crystals or granulomas.
whyWrongByChoice.B: AFE occurs peripartum with acute DIC.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Tumor emboli cause thrombotic inflammation, not granulomas with crystals.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Air emboli do not leave birefringent residue.

questions.stem: Which clinical feature most reliably distinguishes amniotic fluid embolism from septic embolism in a postpartum patient with hypotension and respiratory failure?

explanation.summary: AFE diagnostic criteria include absence of fever plus DIC.
explanation.whyCorrect: Diagnostic criteria for AFE explicitly include absence of fever along with DIC and hypotension/respiratory failure, distinguishing it from septic causes.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Pulmonary edema occurs in both.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Hypoxemia is nonspecific and present in both.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Tachycardia is common to both entities.
whyWrongByChoice.E: CK elevation relates to decompression sickness, not AFE.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman with mitral valve endocarditis develops right-sided weakness and a left splenic abscess. What is the most specific therapy to address the underlying embolic source?

explanation.summary: Septic emboli are treated by treating the underlying infection.
explanation.whyCorrect: No therapy is specific to septic emboli themselves; management consists of treating the infection with antibiotics (with surgical source control as needed).

whyWrongByChoice.A: tPA risks hemorrhage and may transform septic embolic strokes.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Hyperbaric oxygen treats air emboli, not septic.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Oxygen does not address the infectious source.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Anticoagulation is controversial in infected emboli with mycotic aneurysm risk.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old diver surfaces rapidly and develops diffuse body aches, fatigue, and mild dyspnea. Labs reveal elevated creatine kinase. What is the most likely cause of his CK elevation?

explanation.summary: Decompression sickness elevates CK due to muscle ischemia from arterial gas occlusion.
explanation.whyCorrect: After decompression sickness, CK rises due to muscle injury from arteries occluded by nitrogen gas bubbles.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Exertional rhabdo would not produce the characteristic pattern of DCS symptoms.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cardiac barotrauma would elevate troponin, not diffusely elevate CK.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Not relevant to this acute presentation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Hypothermia can raise CK but is not the primary described mechanism.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman with severe acute pancreatitis develops sudden hypoxemia, confusion, and petechiae on her neck. She has no fractures. What is the most likely explanation?

explanation.summary: Fat embolism can rarely arise from non-orthopedic causes including severe pancreatitis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Severe pancreatitis causes fat necrosis that can release fat into circulation, producing fat embolism syndrome even without fracture.

whyWrongByChoice.A: No pregnancy history.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Septic embolism would typically present with fever and bacteremia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: No malignancy history described.
whyWrongByChoice.E: No procedural history mentioned; petechial rash is characteristic of fat embolism.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman 20 minutes postpartum develops hypotension, severe hypoxia, and hemorrhage from her episiotomy site. Which combination of interventions is most appropriate?

explanation.summary: AFE management is supportive: hemodynamic support and blood products for DIC.
explanation.whyCorrect: Management includes CPR/resuscitation, IV fluids, vasopressors if refractory, and blood products (platelets, FFP, cryoprecipitate) for DIC.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Anticoagulation/thrombolytics worsen DIC bleeding.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Not indicated; no air embolism.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Positioning addresses air embolism, not AFE; diuretics worsen hypotension.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Vasodilators worsen hypotension; no infectious source.

questions.stem: A patient had a tunneled central catheter placed yesterday. Today during dressing change, he sits up abruptly and becomes acutely short of breath with hypotension. Cardiac auscultation reveals a 'mill-wheel' murmur. What is the immediate next step after supplemental oxygen?

explanation.summary: Venous air embolism from catheter: left lateral decubitus + oxygen.
explanation.whyCorrect: Left lateral decubitus traps air in the right ventricle apex, preventing migration to pulmonary arteries while oxygen aids bubble resolution.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Right lateral promotes air movement into pulmonary outflow.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Heparin is not indicated for air emboli.
whyWrongByChoice.D: CT delays immediate life-saving positioning.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Epinephrine is not the first-line maneuver for air emboli.

questions.stem: During liposuction, a 45-year-old woman suddenly develops hypoxemia and confusion. Over the next 48 hours, a petechial rash develops on her upper trunk. What is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Liposuction can cause fat embolism from damage to abdominal fat.
explanation.whyCorrect: Non-orthopedic fat embolism can follow liposuction due to disruption of adipose tissue, with classic triad of dyspnea, neurologic change, and petechial rash.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Air embolism would present more acutely without delayed petechial rash.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Thromboembolism lacks petechial rash.
whyWrongByChoice.D: No pregnancy context.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Anaphylaxis presents acutely with urticaria/wheezing, not petechiae 48 hours later.

questions.stem: A patient presents with sudden stroke after shotgun injury to the shoulder 2 weeks ago. Imaging shows metallic fragments in the right ventricle and pulmonary artery. What is the embolic mechanism?

explanation.summary: Bullet/pellet fragment embolization is a recognized foreign body embolism cause.
explanation.whyCorrect: Finely fragmented bullets/pellets can enter vessels and travel through the circulation; per the brick they can embolize from the right ventricle to the lung.

whyWrongByChoice.A: No endocarditis or infection described.
whyWrongByChoice.C: No long bone fracture; scapular injury is not a typical fat embolism source.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Air would not be radiopaque metallic.
whyWrongByChoice.E: No malignancy described.

questions.stem: A motorcyclist develops hypoxemia 48 hours after multiple long-bone fractures. Which intervention is most appropriate?

explanation.summary: Fat embolism management is supportive with oxygen and fluids.
explanation.whyCorrect: No specific therapy exists for fat embolism; management is supportive (oxygen, fluids) with most patients recovering.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Anticoagulation is not indicated for fat emboli.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Thrombolytics target thrombus, not fat.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Steroids are not standard therapy per this source.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Warfarin is not indicated.

questions.stem: A patient with sickle cell disease in vaso-occlusive crisis suddenly develops hypoxia, confusion, and petechiae. ABG shows hypoxemia. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Acute chest syndrome from pneumonia only

choices.label: B
choices.text: Fat embolism from bone marrow necrosis

explanation.summary: Acute sickle cell disease can cause fat embolism from bone marrow necrosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Per the brick, acute sickle cell disease causes fat/bone marrow necrosis leading to fat embolism with the classic triad.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Acute chest syndrome doesn't typically produce petechiae; fat embolism is a recognized precipitant.
whyWrongByChoice.C: PE lacks the petechial rash component.
whyWrongByChoice.D: No obstetric context.
whyWrongByChoice.E: No procedural history.

questions.stem: Comparing embolus types, which statement most accurately differentiates fat embolism from pulmonary thromboembolism?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Fat embolism presents within minutes of injury

choices.label: B
choices.text: Fat embolism produces CT filling defects

choices.label: C
choices.text: Fat embolism typically includes petechial rash

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fat embolism is treated with tPA

explanation.summary: Petechial rash distinguishes fat embolism from thromboembolism.
explanation.whyCorrect: The classic triad (dyspnea + neurologic deficits + red-brown petechial rash on head/neck/anterior trunk) uniquely identifies fat embolism; PE lacks petechiae.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Fat embolism typically presents 24-72 hours after injury, not minutes.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Fat emboli don't produce typical CT filling defects; CT helps rule out PE.
whyWrongByChoice.D: No specific therapy exists; management is supportive.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Hyperbaric oxygen is for air emboli, not fat.

questions.stem: A 29-year-old woman develops sudden cardiorespiratory collapse during cesarean delivery. Histopathology of lung tissue at autopsy would most likely reveal which finding?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fetal squamous cells in pulmonary vasculature

explanation.summary: AFE histopathology shows fetal squamous epithelium in pulmonary vasculature.
explanation.whyCorrect: Amniotic fluid embolism classically shows squamous epithelium from fetal skin and other fetal debris lodged in maternal pulmonary vessels.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Fat globules indicate fat embolism, typically from fractures.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Pure thrombi suggest thromboembolism.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Bacterial vegetations indicate septic emboli.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Talc granulomas suggest IV drug foreign body emboli.
